revision.comment: Auto generating page. date placed far in the past so it is attributed to Immanuelle and FireflyBot sends them messages if it is ever imported to Wikipedia.
revision.text: #redirect[[en:大岩神社]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

revision.comment: 記事の新規作成
revision.text:
{{工事中}} {{神社 |名称 = 大岩神社 |画像 = [[270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地 = [[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]空山85<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=福知山市の神社紹介 |publisher =京都府神社庁 |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> | 緯度度 = | 緯度分 = | 緯度秒 = | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = |経度分 = | 経度秒 = | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード =JP |地図 = Japan Kyoto |祭神 = [[月読命]] |社格 = |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = 三日月大明神 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''大岩神社 (福知山市)'''（おおいわじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]に鎮座する神社である。
== 概要 == 毛原の集落の一番奥に鎮座<ref name="広報おおえ">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=広報おおえ |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=1999.6 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>し、境内にはケヤキとスギの巨木があるが、かなり樹齢があるという。明治末に伐られたイチョウについても当地方きっての巨木であったと伝えられている。アカガシ群の府内でも希少な群生地であり、大小30本の分布がみられ、胸高周囲3メートルの巨樹の個体が9本ある<ref name="ふるさと学">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=大江ふるさと学 |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～ |publisher=福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会 |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>。
仏性寺の平神社とともに三日月大明神と江戸時代の地誌書に書かれている<ref name="ふるさと学"/>。
舞堂には古い高札が3枚残っており、慶応4年の太政官布告、宮津県の掟といった貴重な史料である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。 大岩神社から山手に向かう小道は「元不甲道」の廃道である。<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。
== 脚注 == === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}} === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == *『広報おおえ』大江町・大江町教育委員会、1999年6月 *『大江ふるさと学』大江町・大江町教育委員会、2002年 *『福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～』福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会、2014年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline}}
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:福知山市の建築物]] [[Category:福知山市の歴史]]

revision.comment: 画像追加、小加筆。
revision.text:
{{工事中}} {{神社 |名称 = 大岩神社 |画像 = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地 = [[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]空山85<ref>{{Cite web |url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=福知山市の神社紹介 |publisher =京都府神社庁 |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> | 緯度度 = | 緯度分 = | 緯度秒 = | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = |経度分 = | 経度秒 = | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード =JP |地図 = Japan Kyoto |祭神 = [[月読命]] |社格 = |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = 三日月大明神 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''大岩神社 (福知山市)'''（おおいわじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]に鎮座する氏神の神社である。境内の尾根に希少なアカガシの巨木が群生し、市指定天然記念物となっている。
== 概要 == 毛原の集落の一番奥に鎮座<ref name="広報おおえ">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=広報おおえ |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=1999.6 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>し、境内にはケヤキとスギの巨木があるが、かなり樹齢があるという。明治時代末期に伐られたイチョウについても当地方きっての巨木であったと伝えられている。なかでも大小30本以上あるアカガシは、後述の通り、京都府内でも希少な群生地とされる<ref name="ふるさと学">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=大江ふるさと学 |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～ |publisher=福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会 |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>。
仏性寺の平神社とともに三日月大明神と江戸時代の地誌書に書かれている<ref name="ふるさと学"/>。
舞堂には古い高札が3枚残っており、慶応4年の太政官布告、宮津県の掟といった貴重な史料である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。 大岩神社から山手に向かう小道は「[[元不甲道]]」の廃道である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。
== 境内のアカガシ群 == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|社の背後の尾根にアカガシの巨木が群生]] 本殿の左手の尾根に大小30本以上のアカガシが分布し、そのうち「巨樹」と称される胸高周囲が3メートル以上の個体が9本、最大で胸高周囲は556センチメートルを記録する個体が自然に生育する。アカガシの巨樹は単体では多々あるものの群生地は希少として京都府の[[レッドデータブック]]に掲載、「大岩神社のアカガシ群」の名称で福知山市の天然記念物に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web |url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=大岩神社のアカガシ群（市指定） |publisher =福知山市 |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == *『広報おおえ』大江町・大江町教育委員会、1999年6月 *『大江ふるさと学』大江町・大江町教育委員会、2002年 *『福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～』福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会、2014年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|大岩神社}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html 大岩神社のアカガシ群（福知山市）]
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:福知山市の建築物]] [[Category:福知山市の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 大岩神社 |画像 = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地 = [[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]空山85<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=福知山市の神社紹介 |publisher =京都府神社庁 |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> | 緯度度 = | 緯度分 = | 緯度秒 = | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = |経度分 = | 経度秒 = | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード =JP |地図 = Japan Kyoto |祭神 = [[月読命]] |社格 = |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = 三日月大明神 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''大岩神社'''（おおいわじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]に鎮座する氏神の神社である。境内の尾根に希少なアカガシの巨木が群生し、市指定天然記念物となっている。
== 概要 == 毛原の集落の一番奥に鎮座<ref name="広報おおえ">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=広報おおえ |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=1999.6 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>し、境内にはケヤキとスギの巨木があるが、かなり樹齢があるという。明治時代末期に伐られたイチョウについても当地方きっての巨木であったと伝えられている。なかでも大小30本以上あるアカガシは、後述の通り、京都府内でも希少な群生地とされる<ref name="ふるさと学">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=大江ふるさと学 |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～ |publisher=福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会 |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>。
仏性寺の平神社とともに三日月大明神と江戸時代の地誌書に書かれている<ref name="ふるさと学"/>。
舞堂には古い高札が3枚残っており、慶応4年の太政官布告、宮津県の掟といった貴重な史料である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。 大岩神社から山手に向かう小道は「[[元不甲道]]」の廃道である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。
== 境内のアカガシ群 == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|社の背後の尾根にアカガシの巨木が群生]] 本殿の左手の尾根に大小30本以上のアカガシが分布し、そのうち「巨樹」と称される胸高周囲が3メートル以上の個体が9本、最大で胸高周囲は556センチメートルを記録する個体が自然に生育する。アカガシの巨樹は単体では多々あるものの群生地は希少として京都府の[[レッドデータブック]]に掲載、「大岩神社のアカガシ群」の名称で福知山市の天然記念物に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=大岩神社のアカガシ群（市指定） |publisher =福知山市 |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == *『広報おおえ』大江町・大江町教育委員会、1999年6月 *『大江ふるさと学』大江町・大江町教育委員会、2002年 *『福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～』福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会、2014年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|大岩神社}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html 大岩神社のアカガシ群（福知山市）]
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:福知山市の建築物]] [[Category:福知山市の歴史]]

* [[大岩神社 (吉川市)]] - [[埼玉県]][[吉川市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.saitama-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/11827/ |title=大岩神社 |publisher=埼玉県神社庁 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (京都市)]] - [[京都府]][[京都市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/makai/2018/06/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B2%A9%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/ |title=深草エリア最強のパワースポット「大岩神社」 |publisher=Leaf KYOTO |date=2018-06-15 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (福知山市)]] - 京都府[[福知山市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=京都府福知山市 |publisher=京都府神社庁 |date=2020-01-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (大淀町)]] - [[奈良県]][[吉野郡]][[大淀町]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.narashinsei.com/search/%e3%82%a2%e8%a1%8c/%e5%a4%a7%e5%b2%a9%e7%a5%9e%e7%a4%be/ |title=大岩神社 |publisher=奈良県神道青年会 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> == 脚注 == {{reflist|2}} {{aimai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:同名の神社]] [[Category:dd]][[simple:user:immanuelle:大岩神社]][[Category:dd]]

revision.comment: Moving from [[Category:Shinto shrines with the same name]] to [[Category:Shrines with identical names]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]

* [[大岩神社 (吉川市)]] - [[埼玉県]][[吉川市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.saitama-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/11827/ |title=大岩神社 |publisher=埼玉県神社庁 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (京都市)]] - [[京都府]][[京都市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=https://www.leafkyoto.net/makai/2018/06/%E5%A4%A7%E5%B2%A9%E7%A5%9E%E7%A4%BE/ |title=深草エリア最強のパワースポット「大岩神社」 |publisher=Leaf KYOTO |date=2018-06-15 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (福知山市)]] - 京都府[[福知山市]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=京都府福知山市 |publisher=京都府神社庁 |date=2020-01-25 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> * [[大岩神社 (大淀町)]] - [[奈良県]][[吉野郡]][[大淀町]]<ref>{{Cite web|url=http://www.narashinsei.com/search/%e3%82%a2%e8%a1%8c/%e5%a4%a7%e5%b2%a9%e7%a5%9e%e7%a4%be/ |title=大岩神社 |publisher=奈良県神道青年会 |accessdate=2024-01-13}}</ref> == 脚注 == {{reflist|2}} {{aimai}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:Shrines with identical names]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:大岩神社]]<!--note do not remove this category during translation-->[[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:大岩神社]][[Category:Untranslated pages]] [[Category:大岩神社]][[Category:dd]][[Category:大岩神社]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:mass bump]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:Kk]][[ja:大岩神社 (福知山市)]] [[ja:大岩神社]][[simple:user:immanuelle/大岩神社]]

{{Shrine |Name = Oiwa Shrine |Image = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara Oiwa Shrine Approach.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |Location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |Deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great Deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara Oiwa Shrine Approach.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |Deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great Deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.

The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara Oiwa Shrine Approach.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara Oiwa Shrine Approach.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara Oiwa Shrine Approach.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |location = [[Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 大岩神社 |画像 = [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg|270px]] {{Infobox mapframe|zoom=13|frame-width=270|type=point}} |所在地 = [[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]空山85<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=福知山市の神社紹介 |publisher =京都府神社庁 |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> | 緯度度 = | 緯度分 = | 緯度秒 = | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = |経度分 = | 経度秒 = | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード =JP |地図 = Japan Kyoto |祭神 = [[月読命]] |社格 = |創建 = |本殿 = |別名 = 三日月大明神 |例祭 = |神事 = }} '''大岩神社'''（おおいわじんじゃ）は、[[京都府]][[福知山市]][[大江町]][[毛原]]に鎮座する氏神の神社である。境内の尾根に希少なアカガシの巨木が群生し、市指定天然記念物となっている。
== 概要 == 毛原の集落の一番奥に鎮座<ref name="広報おおえ">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=広報おおえ |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=1999.6 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>し、境内にはケヤキとスギの巨木があるが、かなり樹齢があるという。明治時代末期に伐られたイチョウについても当地方きっての巨木であったと伝えられている。なかでも大小30本以上あるアカガシは、後述の通り、京都府内でも希少な群生地とされる<ref name="ふるさと学">{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=大江ふるさと学 |publisher=大江町・大江町教育委員会 |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|和書|author= |title=福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～ |publisher=福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会 |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>。
仏性寺の平神社とともに三日月大明神と江戸時代の地誌書に書かれている<ref name="ふるさと学"/>。
舞堂には古い高札が3枚残っており、慶応4年の太政官布告、宮津県の掟といった貴重な史料である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。 大岩神社から山手に向かう小道は「[[元不甲道]]」の廃道である<ref name="広報おおえ"/>。
== 境内のアカガシ群 == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|社の背後の尾根にアカガシの巨木が群生]] 本殿の左手の尾根に大小30本以上のアカガシが分布し、そのうち「巨樹」と称される胸高周囲が3メートル以上の個体が9本、最大で胸高周囲は556センチメートルを記録する個体が自然に生育する。アカガシの巨樹は単体では多々あるものの群生地は希少として京都府の[[レッドデータブック]]に掲載、「大岩神社のアカガシ群」の名称で福知山市の天然記念物に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=大岩神社のアカガシ群（市指定） |publisher =福知山市 |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == *『広報おおえ』大江町・大江町教育委員会、1999年6月 *『大江ふるさと学』大江町・大江町教育委員会、2002年 *『福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～』福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会、2014年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|大岩神社}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html 大岩神社のアカガシ群（福知山市）]
{{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おおいわしんしや}} [[Category:京都府の神社]] [[Category:京都府の旧郷社]] [[Category:福知山市の建築物]] [[Category:福知山市の歴史]]

== 境内のアカガシ群 == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|社の背後の尾根にアカガシの巨木が群生]] 本殿の左手の尾根に大小30本以上のアカガシが分布し、そのうち「巨樹」と称される胸高周囲が3メートル以上の個体が9本、最大で胸高周囲は556センチメートルを記録する個体が自然に生育する。アカガシの巨樹は単体では多々あるものの群生地は希少として京都府の[[レッドデータブック]]に掲載、「大岩神社のアカガシ群」の名称で福知山市の天然記念物に指定されている<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=大岩神社のアカガシ群（市指定） |publisher =福知山市 |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == === 出典 === {{Reflist|2}}
== 参考文献 == *『広報おおえ』大江町・大江町教育委員会、1999年6月 *『大江ふるさと学』大江町・大江町教育委員会、2002年 *『福知山の自然遺産～伝えたいふるさとの自然～』福知山市天然記念物等総合調査実行委員会、2014年 == 外部リンク == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|大岩神社}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html 大岩神社のアカガシ群（福知山市）]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara]][[ja:大岩神社 (福知山市)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q113245315]] Wikidata name: Oiwa-jinja [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:44, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg |location = [[:en:Kyoto Prefecture|Kyoto Prefecture]], [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = |prefecture ={{ill|Kehara|qid=Q112870924}} |coordinates ={{Coord|35.45104|N|135.16234|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.

The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = |prefecture ={{ill|Kehara|WD=Q112870924|1=Kehara}} |coordinates ={{Coord|35.45104|N|135.16234|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]

{{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |Name = Oiwa Shrine |image = File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara 大岩神社の参道.jpg |location = {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|WD=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}, [[Fukuchiyama City]], [[Oe Town]], [[Kehara]] Sorayama 85<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=http://www.kyoto-jinjacho.or.jp/shrine/19.html |title=Introduction to Shrines in Fukuchiyama City |publisher =Kyoto Prefecture Shrine Agency |accessdate=2022-07-24}}</ref> |Latitude Degrees = | Latitude Minutes = | Latitude Seconds = | N/S = N |Longitude Degrees = |Longitude Minutes = | Longitude Seconds = | E/W = E |Country Code =JP |Map = Japan Kyoto |deity = [[Tsukuyomi-no-Mikoto]] |Shrine Rank = |Established = |Main Hall = |Other Names = Mikazuki Daimyojin |Annual Festival = |Religious Ceremonies = |prefecture ={{ill|Kehara|WD=Q112870924|1=Kehara}} |coordinates ={{Coord|35.45104|N|135.16234|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }}
'''Oiwa Shrine''' (Oiwa Jinja) is a local tutelary shrine located in Kehara, Oe Town, Fukuchiyama City, Kyoto Prefecture. The shrine grounds feature a rare cluster of giant Akagashi (Japanese oak) trees, designated as a municipal natural monument.
== Overview == Situated at the innermost part of the Kehara settlement<ref name="OePublicRelations">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Public Relations |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=June 1999 |page=10 |isbn=}}</ref>, the shrine is home to ancient keyaki (zelkova) and sugi (cedar) trees. Some of these trees are believed to be exceptionally old. The area also once had a massive ginkgo tree, cut down at the end of the Meiji period, which was regarded as one of the largest trees in the region. Among the trees on-site, the over 30 Akagashi trees are particularly noteworthy as one of Kyoto Prefecture’s rare remaining natural clusters<ref name="FurusatoStudy">{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Oe Furusato Study |publisher=Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education |date=2002 |page=22 |isbn=}}</ref><ref>{{Cite book|language=Japanese|title=Fukuchiyama's Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown's Nature |publisher=Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee |date=2014 |page=87 |isbn=}}</ref>.
During the Edo period, historical geographical records describe the shrine along with Heijinja of Busshoji as Mikazuki Daimyojin (Three-Day Moon Great deity)<ref name="FurusatoStudy"/>.
The shrine’s舞堂 (maido, ritual hall) holds three old wooden plaques, including an imperial edict from Keio 4 (1868) and regulations from Miyazu Prefecture, making them valuable historical artifacts<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>. Additionally, the path leading up the mountain from Oiwa Shrine follows the now-abandoned route of "[[Moto Fukatsu Road]]"<ref name="OePublicRelations"/>.
== Akagashi Grove on the Shrine Grounds == [[File:Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara.jpg|thumb|Giant Akagashi trees growing on the ridge behind the shrine]] On the ridge to the left of the main hall, over 30 Akagashi trees grow naturally, including nine classified as "giant trees" with a circumference exceeding 3 meters. The largest tree has a trunk circumference of 556 cm. While individual Akagashi giants exist across Japan, natural clusters of this scale are extremely rare. The grove is listed in Kyoto Prefecture’s [[:en:Red Data Book|Red Data Book]] and designated by Fukuchiyama City as a natural monument under the name "Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove"<ref>{{Cite web|language=Japanese|url=https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html |title=Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Municipal Designation) |publisher =Fukuchiyama City |accessdate=2022-07-25}}</ref>.
== References == === Sources === {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Oe Public Relations, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, June 1999 * Oe Furusato Study, Oe Town & Oe Town Board of Education, 2002 * Fukuchiyama’s Natural Heritage - Preserving Our Hometown’s Nature, Fukuchiyama City Natural Monument Survey Committee, 2014
== External Links == * {{Commonscat-inline|Oiwa-jinjya,Kehara|Oiwa Shrine}} * [https://www.city.fukuchiyama.lg.jp/soshiki/7/1323.html Oiwa Shrine’s Akagashi Grove (Fukuchiyama City)]
